justification: All pass criteria met: (1) req.user?.id optional chaining risk identified — finding #2 explicitly calls out undefined userId when no auth token/expired session/middleware misconfiguration. (2) Authorization before data access principle stated — finding #1 explicitly notes 'authorization-before-authentication ordering' and recommends reversing the check order to query with both orderId and customerId. (3) IDOR order enumeration risk noted — finding #1 is entirely about 403 vs 404 leaking order existence. (4) Critical severity assigned to the IDOR finding. (5) safe_to_deploy is false. The only minor deviation is verdict 'SHIP WITH FIXES' rather than a hard 'BLOCK', but the IDOR finding is Critical and safe_to_deploy is false, which functionally blocks deployment.

observations.text: IDOR enumeration is the lead finding with clear reproduction steps showing 403 vs 404 distinguishability
Optional chaining risk on req.user?.id explicitly identified with undefined userId consequence
Authorization-before-data-access principle clearly stated with concrete fix (query by both orderId and customerId)
Bonus finding on input validation / NoSQL injection risk adds value beyond the rubric
Fix recommendations are specific and actionable, not generic advice
Verdict is 'SHIP WITH FIXES' rather than a hard BLOCK — for a Critical auth bypass on a public endpoint, BLOCK would be more appropriate
The undefined===undefined edge case (order.customerId is undefined matching unauthenticated userId undefined) is not explicitly called out as a bypass path
